# Things I worked on this and last week
1. Redid convergence rate analysis for Gaussian + AWGN channel for $I_t = \alpha_t \mathcal F(X)+ \beta_t X$ interpolant. 
	- $\epsilon_t = 0$ (ODE flow) makes converge rate tight.
> Eric: There's something interesting about the lifted space that it has this spurious fixed point. 
2. Implemented Flow Map Matching (consistency model) version of SCSI algorithm.
	- Training is faster (wall-clock time) on MLP but slower on UNet.
	- In general, this makes the E-step the bottleneck instead of the M step. 
> Eric: Do the Flow Map this way https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.18825. However, leave this for later. Let's try to get just the ODE to work, and then make it more efficient...
3. 2D Toy Problems.
	1. Play around with conditional vs joint interpolant.
		- Proof that you need a separate noise from ODE flow $\Phi(z | y)$ and interpolant $I_t = \alpha_t z' + \beta_t x$.
		- Supervised: Joint seems to perform always worse...
	2. (Something to look at..?) Redo convergence analysis in these settings?
> Eric: Proof of converge is interesting in conditional lifted version. And the joint interpolant doesn't actually sample the conditional distribution.
4. MNIST
	1. Setting up HPC.
	2. Trying to get MRA to work in supervised setting.

Discussion
- What should we focus on more specifically?
	1. Toy problems to understanding conditional vs joint interpolant
	2. MRA MNIST

> Let's prioritize the questions we're asking
> - **Let's focus on MRA MNIST**
> 	- Need some sort of measure of progress on the EM loop. E.g. plot the histogram of the marginals 
> 	- What's the allowed priors here?
> 		- Give it an example of each digit, allow it to cheat>
> - Read literature in the area

____

# Proof: You need two noises
From [[2026-04-28 Why we need separate noise sources]]

Eric proposed a change to the lifted SCSI algorithm
```pseudo
\begin{algorithm}
\begin{algorithmic}
\State $y \sim p_{\text{corrupt}}$
\State $z,\textcolor{blue}{z'}\sim \mathcal N(0, \mathbb I)$, $t \sim [0,1]$
\State $x_{\text{fake}} \leftarrow \Phi(\textcolor{blue}{z'} | y)$
\State $y_{\text{fake}} \leftarrow \mathcal F(x_{\text{fake}})$
\State $I_t \leftarrow \alpha_t z + \beta_t x_{\text{fake}}$, $\dot I_t \leftarrow \dot \alpha_t z + \dot \beta_t x_{\text{fake}}$
\State $\mathcal L = \| b_t(I_t | y_{\text{fake}}) - \dot I_t\|^2 $
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}
```
Before there were no blue $z'$, only $z$. This is because if you use the same $z$, there is a fixed point at $\hat b_t = 0$.

***Proof (fixed point at $\hat b_t = 0$):***
Consider the interpolant

$$
\begin{align}
I_t^{(k)} & = (1-t) Z + t X^{(k-1)}\\
\dot I_t^{(k)} & = X^{(k-1)} - Z
\end{align}
$$

where $X$ is construct from the ODE $dX_t= b^{(k-1)}_t(X_t) dt$, where $X_{t=0} = Z$ and I'm labeling $X_{t=1}=: X^{(k-1)}$. Note we've fixed the RNG for $Z$, meaning it's the same $Z$ in $I_t$ and in the initial condition for the ODE.

Using the MSE loss

$$
\begin{align}
\mathcal  L(\hat b) & = \mathbb E[(\hat b_t(I_t | Y) - \dot I_t)^2]\\
& = \mathbb E\Big[\big(\hat b_t((1-t)Z + t X\; |\; Y) - (X-Z)\big)^2\Big]
\end{align}
$$

you can see that $\hat b_t = 0$ is the trivial minimizer. This is because when $b_t = 0$, then $X_{t=0} = X_{t=1}$, therefore $X_{t=0} = Z = X$. This kills the right term leaving you with

$$
\begin{align}
\mathcal L(\hat b) & = \mathbb E[\hat b_t(Z|Y)^2] \\
\implies \hat b_t & = 0
\end{align}
$$

***Proof ($z'$ fixes this problem)***
> 🙋 Looking for critique of this proof

*Assumption (AWGN Channel):*  $Y = F(X) = X + \sigma W$, where $W \sim \mathcal N(0, \mathbb I)$.

$$
\begin{align}
I_t^{(k)} & = (1-t) Z + t X^{(k-1)}\\\\
& = (1-t) Z + t Z' & \hat b_t = 0
\end{align}
$$

Consider the variational derivative $\frac{d}{d\epsilon}\mathcal L(\hat b + \epsilon h)  \Big|_{\epsilon = 0, \hat b =0}$ . For $\hat b = 0$ to be a local minimizer, this quantity must be zero $\forall \eta$

$$
\begin{align}
\mathcal L(\hat b + \epsilon h) & = \mathbb E[(b(I_t | Y) + \epsilon h(I_t | Y)  - \dot I_t)^2]\\
\frac{d}{d\epsilon } \mathcal L(\hat b + \epsilon h)& =  \mathbb E[2 (b(I_t | Y) + \epsilon h(I_t | Y)  - \dot I_t) (h(I_t | Y))]\\
\frac{d}{d\epsilon } \mathcal L(\hat b + \epsilon h) \Big|_{\epsilon =0, b=0} & = \mathbb E[2 \dot I_t \, h(I_t | Y)] \\
& = 2 \mathbb E[ (X - Z) h(I_t | Y)]\\
& = 2 \mathbb E[(Z' - Z) h(I_t | Y)]\\
& = 2 \mathbb E[\mathbb E[Z' - Z | I_t, Y] \, h(I_t |Y)]
\end{align}
$$

So $\hat b = 0$ is a critical point i.f.f. $\mathbb E[Z' - Z | I_t , Y] = 0$.

This is computable because they're jointly Gaussian. Let $A = Z'-Z$. The conditional expectation becomes 

$$
\begin{align}
\mathbb E[A | I_t , Y] =  \frac{I_t [(1 -t)+ \sigma^2(1-2t)] - Y[1-t]}{(-1+t)^2 + \sigma^2 (1-2t + 2t^2)}
\end{align}
$$

Ways to get this to be zero
- Set $t$ s.t. LHS and RHS are both zero, which is not possible, Except when $\sigma = 0$ (AWGN channel is off).
- Find set where $I_t, Y$ coincide according to equation above. However this set is measure zero, so you wouldn't hit it in practice.
$\blacksquare$

> Eric has intuition that you can do this w/o needing to assume $Y = F_{awgn}(X)$, there's a generalization here... But the result fits his intuition here.
___
# MNIST - Supervised Setting

## AWGN Channel 
Just tried this!
![[2026-05-01 awgn mnist transformer.png]]
This is after just 1 epoch!!! Using DiT architecture. Concat clean image and noisy image.
I think the future is we should just use tranformer

> Eric: forget about UNets use SiT (this is just DiT) or DINO. Look into SiT

#### Extra Thoughts...
Was briefly chatting w/ Alex (Ali) and we were talking about various losses of diffusion models. Consider the diffusion interpolant on uniformly translated data
$$
\begin{align}
I_t = \alpha_t Z + \beta_tG\cdot X
\end{align}
$$
The optimal drift is given by $b_t(x) = \mathbb E[\dot I_t | I_t = x]$. If you substitute it back in you can rearrange this to either get a drift which depends on reconstruction $\mathbb E[G\cdot X | I_t = x]$ or denoiser $\mathbb E[Z | I_t =x]$. You can think of these as posterior mean estimates. 

Consider the reconstruction loss
$$
\begin{align}
\mathcal L(\hat f) = \mathbb E[(\hat f_t(I_t) - G \cdot X)^2] \approx \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N (\hat f_{t_i}(\alpha_{t_i} z_i + \beta_{t_i}g_i \cdot x_i) - g_i \cdot x_i)^2
\end{align}
$$
vs the denoiser loss
$$
\begin{align}
\mathcal L(\hat f) = \mathbb E[(\hat f_t(I_t) - Z)^2] \approx \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^N (\hat f_{t_i}(\alpha_{t_i} z_i + \beta_{t_i} g_i \cdot x_i)- z_i)^2
\end{align}
$$
Alex Ali did some extra experiments and finds that the reconstruction loss does way better than the denoiser loss.
## MRA Channel
1. I think it's becoming increasingly clear that $\pi^{(0)}$ (initial guess) needs to be good
2. Getting the supervised method to train is a bit difficult. 

![](data:image/png;base64,)
Supervised setting. UNet w/ circular padding. Trained for 2000 epochs.

> Eric: Make plots that show intermediate points in flow time. The observations we have are very close to $\mathcal F(X)$; why are learning to transport $Z \to \mathcal F(X)$ instead of $Z \to X$...? 
> Eric maybe thinks there are two phases of training. The first converges to to a fixed point at $\mathcal F(X)$, and then it starts to converge to $X$.
